semantic_journey: Arabic skeleton thn vs Latin ml — zero consonant overlap. Translation equivalence: every grain-farming civilization independently names its grinding tool.
key_insight: Same meaning but incompatible consonant structures — translation, not cognate.

semantic_journey: Arabic severance/amputation (j-dh-m) and Gothic moral goodness occupy entirely unrelated semantic fields.
key_insight: Skeleton match is coincidental; no semantic bridge between cutting and goodness.

semantic_journey: Arabic n-d-s = to thrust/push against with force. Gothic andastauin = to stand against/resist. Both encode directed physical opposition — force meeting resistance.
key_insight: Physical opposition concept preserved: thrusting against = standing against.

semantic_journey: Arabic b-n-s = to stretch/lie flat. Gothic abans = even/level/flat. Both encode the concept of an unvarying flat surface.
key_insight: Flat surface description preserved: stretching flat = level/even.

semantic_journey: Flooding/submersion vs timber construction have no plausible semantic contact. The skeleton overlap is coincidental.
key_insight: No semantic bridge between overwhelming/flooding and building/constructing.

semantic_journey: Arabic d-l-k = to rub/massage/polish by friction. Old English dolc = wound/scar/gash. Concrete-to-result drift: rubbing -> abrasion -> wound/scar. Arabic covers the full spectrum of friction; OE narrowed to the extreme result.
key_insight: Friction action -> its extreme result: rubbing becomes the wound left by rubbing.

semantic_journey: Arabic s-l-t = forceful cutting/striking through resistance (also: power/sultan). Old English slit = a cut/incision. Arabic later abstracted to power/authority; OE preserved the literal blade-sense.
key_insight: Forceful cutting preserved literally in OE while Arabic abstracted to authority/power.

semantic_journey: Sound-making (bell) vs ground-covering plant have no semantic connection. Gras is inherited pan-Germanic vocabulary, not a borrowing.
key_insight: Bell-ringing and grass occupy entirely different semantic fields; skeleton overlap is coincidental.

semantic_journey: Arabic j-l-d family = skin/hide; thick-hided robust animal. Old Irish geilid = to eat/graze (of livestock). Metonymic path: the thick-hided beast -> that beast defining action (grazing).
key_insight: Tough-hided livestock animal -> the act that defines livestock: grazing.

semantic_journey: Both are independent borrowings from Latin candela. Arabic borrowed via Greek/trade; Old Irish via Christian missionaries. The perfect c-n-d-l match reflects a shared Latin source, not Arabic-Irish genealogy.
key_insight: Shared Latin loanword, not an Arabic-origin cognate. Classifying this as a discovery would be a methodological error.
